purpose: Reference catalog for establishing a requirements hierarchy in a radioactive waste management Requirement Management System (RMS). Intended for later use in database design, data seeding, validation rules, and controlled vocabularies.
notes: The model separates needs, functional architecture, solution/design, implementation, and compliance evidence.
Examples are illustrative and should be tailored to the facility type, waste stream, national regulations, and project phase.
The level structure follows a practical interpretation of the EURAD-style hierarchy discussed in the project context.

levels.level_name: External High-Level Goals, Needs, and Expectations
levels.short_definition: Top-level external requirements that explain why the programme or facility exists and what must ultimately be achieved.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level captures the highest-level goals, needs, and expectations coming from external stakeholders such as the state, regulator, society, waste owners, or international safety principles. These are not yet detailed design requirements. They define the overall purpose, safety intent, and boundary conditions for the programme.
levels.typical_question_answered: Why does this programme or facility exist, and what top-level outcome must it achieve?

example_requirements.title: Protection of people and the environment
example_requirements.statement: Radioactive waste shall be managed in a manner that protects people and the environment in the short and long term.

example_requirements.title: Regulatory compliance
example_requirements.statement: The waste management programme shall comply with applicable national regulatory requirements.

example_requirements.title: Long-term safety objective
example_requirements.statement: The disposal system shall provide an acceptable level of post-closure safety.

levels.database_hint: Use this level for the highest originating requirements. These usually have no parent requirement inside the project hierarchy, but they often link strongly to legal or policy source documents.

levels.level_name: Programme or System Goals and Principles
levels.short_definition: Internalized programme-level goals and principles derived from L0 requirements.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level translates external high-level expectations into programme or system goals that the implementing organization can manage directly. It often includes safety strategy, management principles, design philosophy, and programme objectives.
levels.typical_question_answered: What main goals and principles will the programme adopt in order to satisfy the external expectations?

example_requirements.title: Passive safety principle
example_requirements.statement: Post-closure safety shall rely primarily on passive safety features.

example_requirements.title: Traceability principle
example_requirements.statement: Waste packages and associated records shall remain traceable throughout the facility lifecycle.

example_requirements.title: Exposure minimization
example_requirements.statement: Occupational exposure shall be managed according to optimization principles.

levels.database_hint: These are usually derived from L0 and may serve as parents for functions, characteristics, and performance targets.

levels.short_definition: Statements describing what the system, subsystem, process, or barrier must do.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level captures functional requirements. A function describes the role or action that an element of the system must perform. Functional requirements do not yet fully define design choices; they describe required behavior or capability.
levels.typical_question_answered: What must the system or element do?

example_requirements.title: Waste characterization
example_requirements.statement: The facility shall characterize incoming waste before acceptance.

example_requirements.title: Containment function
example_requirements.statement: The waste package shall limit radionuclide release during handling and storage.

example_requirements.title: Traceability function
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall maintain traceable links between requirements, source documents, and evidence.

levels.database_hint: Allocate these requirements to functional architecture elements, processes, SSCs, or organizational responsibilities.

levels.short_definition: Statements describing how well something must perform or what qualitative properties it must possess.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level captures non-functional characteristics such as reliability, maintainability, durability, robustness, information integrity, security, usability, auditability, and resilience. These requirements shape quality expectations without necessarily defining a specific design solution.
levels.typical_question_answered: How well must it work, or what qualities must it have?

example_requirements.title: Audit trail
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall provide a complete and tamper-evident audit trail for requirement changes.

example_requirements.title: Data integrity
example_requirements.statement: Critical waste records shall be protected against unauthorized modification.

example_requirements.title: Maintainability
example_requirements.statement: Monitoring equipment shall be maintainable without unacceptable exposure to workers.

levels.database_hint: Keep these separate from functional requirements when possible. They are often later translated into measurable quality targets at L3b.

levels.short_definition: Measurable targets showing how successfully a function must be achieved.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level turns functions into measurable targets. The requirement should ideally contain objective, verifiable, and testable criteria such as ranges, thresholds, capacities, response times, or performance limits.
levels.typical_question_answered: How much, how often, how fast, or to what limit must the function be achieved?

example_requirements.title: Incoming waste screening performance
example_requirements.statement: The waste acceptance process shall verify 100 percent of incoming packages against the approved acceptance criteria before emplacement.

example_requirements.title: Ventilation performance
example_requirements.statement: The ventilation system shall maintain airborne contamination below the approved operational limit under normal operating conditions.

example_requirements.title: Query traceability performance
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall return requirement-to-evidence traceability results for approved users within the defined system response time.

levels.database_hint: This level is ideal for linking to verification cases, acceptance criteria, and performance indicators.

levels.short_definition: Measurable targets for non-functional or quality-related requirements.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level transforms quality characteristics into measurable and verifiable targets. If L2b says a system shall be reliable, L3b explains how reliable it must be, how availability will be measured, or what threshold demonstrates compliance.
levels.typical_question_answered: What measurable criterion shows that the desired quality has been achieved?

example_requirements.title: Availability target
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall achieve the approved minimum service availability for business-critical requirement records.

example_requirements.title: Backup target
example_requirements.statement: Approved requirement data shall be backed up at the defined frequency and recoverable within the approved recovery objective.

example_requirements.title: Audit completeness target
example_requirements.statement: The system shall retain a complete revision history for every approved requirement record.

levels.database_hint: Use this level for measurable non-functional criteria that can later be verified through tests, audits, or configuration reviews.

levels.short_definition: Consolidated input requirements that a designer must use when developing a solution.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level gathers all relevant functional requirements, quality requirements, performance targets, constraints, loads, environmental conditions, and regulatory conditions that form the input set for design work. It tells the designer what the design must satisfy.
levels.typical_question_answered: What inputs, boundary conditions, loads, and targets must the design satisfy?

example_requirements.title: Waste package design inputs
example_requirements.statement: The waste package design shall account for the approved waste inventory, handling loads, thermal conditions, chemical environment, and long-term containment targets.

example_requirements.title: RMS design inputs
example_requirements.statement: The RMS design shall address approved user roles, audit requirements, access control rules, retention rules, integration needs, and traceability obligations.

example_requirements.title: Repository barrier design inputs
example_requirements.statement: The barrier design shall consider approved groundwater chemistry, mechanical loads, temperature evolution, and required performance targets.

levels.database_hint: These requirements often sit at the transition between the needs domain and the solution domain. They should be linked to allocations, constraints, and assumptions.

levels.short_definition: Specifications describing the chosen design solution or design outputs.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level describes the design result. It includes the selected materials, dimensions, architecture, interfaces, component specifications, data structures, and other output specifications that define the chosen solution.
levels.typical_question_answered: What design has been produced in order to satisfy the design inputs?
typical_sources: Civil, mechanical, electrical, and IT design outputs

example_requirements.title: Container material specification
example_requirements.statement: The disposal container shall use the approved material grade and minimum wall thickness defined by the reference design.

example_requirements.title: RMS data model specification
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall implement the approved entity structure for requirements, sources, allocations, verification records, and revision history.

example_requirements.title: Barrier geometry specification
example_requirements.statement: The engineered barrier shall conform to the approved geometry and installation envelope specified in the reference design.

levels.database_hint: These records usually have strong links to design documents, drawings, schemas, interface specifications, and reference models.

levels.level_name: Production, Construction, Implementation, and Verification Specifications
levels.short_definition: Requirements describing how the approved design will be produced, implemented, controlled, inspected, and verified.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level addresses implementation. It includes fabrication rules, construction methods, work instructions, acceptance inspection rules, commissioning conditions, test plans, installation tolerances, software deployment rules, and implementation QA/QC controls.
levels.typical_question_answered: How will the approved solution be built, configured, installed, inspected, and verified?

example_requirements.title: Fabrication tolerances
example_requirements.statement: Waste package fabrication shall comply with the approved dimensional tolerances and inspection hold points.

example_requirements.title: RMS deployment control
example_requirements.statement: The RMS shall be deployed, tested, and released in accordance with the approved configuration and change-control procedure.

example_requirements.title: Barrier installation control
example_requirements.statement: Barrier installation shall follow the approved emplacement procedure and quality control checks.

levels.database_hint: This level commonly links to procedures, work packages, inspection plans, test specifications, and verification cases.

levels.level_name: Implemented Product Documentation and Compliance Evidence
levels.short_definition: Records showing what was actually built, implemented, tested, accepted, and demonstrated.
levels.detailed_explanation: This level does not define what should be done; it records what was actually done and what evidence demonstrates compliance. Typical records include as-built documentation, test reports, inspection results, validation results, material certificates, audit records, and regulatory submission evidence.
levels.typical_question_answered: What was actually implemented, and what evidence shows that it complies?

example_requirements.title: As-built evidence
example_requirements.statement: As-built documentation shall be generated and retained for approved safety-significant structures, systems, and components.

example_requirements.title: Verification evidence retention
example_requirements.statement: Verification results demonstrating requirement compliance shall be retained in the approved evidence repository.

example_requirements.title: Regulatory submission evidence
example_requirements.statement: Compliance evidence required for licensing submissions shall be compiled, reviewed, and retained in the approved records system.

levels.database_hint: Although often document-heavy, this level should still be linkable to requirements, verification cases, baselines, and approval decisions.

notes: Store level_code in each requirement record.
Keep this catalog as a controlled reference table.
Use the example requirements only as seed examples, not as final approved project requirements.
